  • 不得白日升天 - '白日升天' is a Taoist concept referring to ascending to heaven during one's lifetime.
  • 见召为少室山伯 - '见召' means 'was summoned,' and '少室山伯' refers to a position or title related to the Shaoshi Mountains.
  • 将行在即翌日沐浴加冠衣焚香而卒 - '将行在即' means 'just before his departure,' '翌日' means 'the next day,' '沐浴' means 'took a bath,' '加冠衣' means 'put on ceremonial robes,' and '焚香' means 'burned incense.'
  • 谥曰升先生 - '谥' means 'posthumous title,' and '升先生' is the posthumous title given to the person.
  • 益州老父 - '益州' refers to the ancient region of Yizhou (modern-day Sichuan), and '老父' means 'an old man.'
  • 唐则天末年 - '唐则天' refers to Empress Wu Zetian of the Tang Dynasty, and '末年' means 'in the later years.'
  • 擕一药壶于城中卖药 - '擕' means 'carried,' '一药壶' means 'a medicine pot,' and '于城中卖药' means 'selling medicine in the city.'
  • 得钱即转济贫乏 - '得钱' means 'received money,' and '转济贫乏' means 'gave it to the poor.'
  • 自常不食时即饮浄水 - '自常' means 'usually,' '不食' means 'did not eat,' and '浄水' means 'pure water.'
  • 每遇有识者必告之 - '每遇' means 'every time he met,' '有识者' means 'wise people,' and '必告之' means 'he would tell them.'
  • 夫人一身便如一国也 - '夫人' means 'a person,' '一身' means 'the body,' and '便如一国' means 'is like a country.'
  • 人之心即帝王也 - '人之心' means 'the heart of a person,' and '帝王' means 'emperor or king.'
  • 傍列脏腑即内辅也 - '傍列' means 'arranged around,' '脏腑' means 'internal organs,' and '内辅' means 'internal ministers.'
  • 外张九窍即外臣也 - '外张' means 'externally open,' '九窍' means 'nine orifices' (eyes, ears, nose, mouth, etc.), and '外臣' means 'external officials.'
  • 但凡欲身之无病必须先正其心 - '但凡' means 'if one wants,' '欲身之无病' means 'to be free of illness,' and '必须先正其心' means 'must first correct the heart.'
  • 不使乱求不使狂思不使嗜欲不使迷惑 - '不使' means 'do not let,' '乱求' means 'seeking in disorder,' '狂思' means 'wild thoughts,' '嗜欲' means 'desires,' and '迷惑' means 'confusion.'
  • 则心先无病 - '则' means 'then,' and '心先无病' means 'the heart will first be free of illness.'
  • 内辅之脏腑虽有病不难疗也 - '内辅之脏腑' means 'the internal organs,' and '虽有病不难疗也' means 'even if they are sick, they are not difficult to treat.'
  • 外之九窍 - '外之九窍' means 'the external nine orifices.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦王世民 - Refers to Li Shimin, the second emperor of the Tang Dynasty, known as Emperor Taizong.
  • 房龄 - Refers to Fang Xuanling, a prominent statesman and advisor to Emperor Taizong.
  • 贞观九年 - Refers to the ninth year of the Zhenguan era, which was 635 AD.
  • 润州茅山 - Refers to the Wudang Mountains in Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province, known for their Taoist temples.
  • 太平观 - Refers to a Taoist temple named 'Tai Ping Guan.'
  • 少室山 - Refers to the Shaoshi Mountains, located in Henan Province, known for their Taoist significance.
  • 升先生 - Refers to the posthumous title given to the person, meaning 'Rising Xuan Master.'
  • 益州老父 - Refers to an old man from the ancient region of Yizhou.
  • 唐则天 - Refers to Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
  • 九窍 - Refers to the nine orifices of the human body, which are significant in traditional Chinese medicine and philosophy.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes a covert visit by Li Shimin and his advisor Fang Xuanling to a Taoist master.
  • This phrase indicates that Li Shimin is about to become a ruler who brings peace and prosperity.
  • This is a polite expression used to bid farewell.
  • This phrase describes the elevation of Li Shimin's position after he ascended the throne.
  • This phrase indicates that Li Shimin firmly requested to retire to a hermitage.
  • This sentence describes the establishment of a Taoist temple in 635 AD.
  • This phrase indicates that an imperial edict was issued to console and encourage the Taoist master.
  • This phrase refers to the criteria for becoming a Taoist immortal.
  • This phrase suggests that the person had a minor wrongdoing involving a child.
  • This phrase indicates that the person could not ascend to heaven during his lifetime.
  • This phrase describes the person being summoned to a position related to the Shaoshi Mountains.
  • This phrase describes the person's final actions before his death.
  • This phrase indicates that the person was given a posthumous title.
  • This phrase introduces an old man from the ancient region of Yizhou.
  • This phrase sets the time period to the later years of Empress Wu Zetian's reign.
  • This phrase describes the old man carrying a medicine pot and selling medicine in the city.
  • This phrase indicates that the old man gave the money to the poor.
  • This phrase describes the old man's habit of drinking pure water instead of eating.
  • This phrase indicates that the old man would tell wise people about his experiences.
  • This phrase describes the old man's philosophy on the body and the heart.
  • This phrase emphasizes the importance of correcting the heart to avoid illness.
  • This phrase advises against seeking in disorder, having wild thoughts, desiring excessively, and being confused.
  • This phrase indicates that the heart will first be free of illness if these conditions are avoided.
  • This phrase suggests that even if the internal organs are sick, they are not difficult to treat.
  • This phrase refers to the external nine orifices of the human body.

[298]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 亦无由受病矣 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'there is no way to get sick,' implying that if the body is in harmony, it cannot be affected by illness.
  • 况药亦有君臣有佐有使 - Refers to the hierarchical relationship in traditional Chinese medicine, where '君' (sovereign) is the primary herb, '臣' (minister) is the secondary herb, and '佐' (assistant) and '使' (envoy) are supporting herbs.
  • 茍或攻其病君先臣次然后用佐用使 - Describes the proper order of administering herbs: the sovereign herb is used first, followed by the minister, and then the assistant and envoy herbs, emphasizing the importance of hierarchy in treatment.
  • 自然合其宜 - Means 'naturally fits the right conditions,' suggesting that the treatment should be appropriate for the patient's condition.
  • 如以佐之药用之以使使之药用之以佐 - Describes a situation where the assistant herb is used as the envoy herb, and the envoy herb is used as the assistant herb, leading to a reversal of roles, which is a metaphor for improper treatment.
  • 必自乱也 - Means 'it will inevitably become chaotic,' indicating that such a reversal will lead to confusion and failure in treatment.
  • 又何能攻人之病哉 - Means 'how could it possibly treat the patient's illness?' Emphasizes the futility of improper treatment.
  • 此又象国家治人也 - Uses a metaphor to compare the proper administration of medicine to the governance of a state, suggesting that just as a state needs a proper hierarchy, so too does a treatment.
  • 老夫用药常以此为念 - Means 'I always keep this in mind when prescribing medicine,' indicating that the speaker is reflecting on the importance of proper hierarchy in treatment.
  • 每遇人一身君不君臣不臣使九窍之斜悉纳其病 - Describes a situation where the body's organs (represented by the '九窍' or nine orifices) are in disarray, leading to illness, using a metaphor to describe the body's dysfunction.
  • 以至于良医自逃名药不效犹不知治身之病 - Means 'even a good doctor would flee from fame if the medicine is ineffective, yet the person still does not know how to treat their own illness,' suggesting that the patient's lack of self-awareness leads to ineffective treatment.
  • 悲夫 - Expression of sorrow or pity, often used to conclude a passage with a sense of regret.
  • 士君子记之 - Means 'let the scholars and gentlemen remember this,' indicating that the speaker is addressing an audience of educated individuals.
  • 谓众人曰老夫罪已满矣今却归岛上俄化一白飞去衣与药壶并没于水永寻不见 - Describes the speaker's final words to the people, stating that their sins are complete and they are returning to an island, where they transform into a white bird and fly away, leaving their clothes and medicine pot behind in the water, a metaphorical description of death and transformation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 锦川 - Proper noun referring to a specific place, likely a river or a scenic area, used metaphorically in the text to describe a place of transformation.
  • 老夫 - Respectful term used by an older person to refer to themselves, common in Classical Chinese texts.
  • 九窍 - Refers to the nine orifices of the body, traditionally associated with the nine organs in Chinese medicine, a key concept in the text's metaphorical description of the body's dysfunction.
  • 白 - Metaphorical description of a white bird, likely a crane or a swan, which symbolizes transformation and transcendence in Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 锦川 - Proper noun referring to a specific place, likely a river or a scenic area.
  • 老夫 - Respectful term used by an older person to refer to themselves.
  • 九窍 - Term referring to the nine orifices of the body, traditionally associated with the nine organs in Chinese medicine.
  • 白 - Metaphorical description of a white bird, likely a crane or a swan, which symbolizes transformation and transcendence in Chinese culture.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 进士 - Scholar-official who passed the imperial examinations, indicating a high social status.

  • 予之奉邀 - I invite you to come.
  • 愿事君子 - Wants to serve a gentleman.
  • 此亦防数前定不可免也 - This is also predestined by fate and cannot be avoided.
  • 生拜谢 - Cui Sheng bowed and thanked him.
  • 顾左右 - Looked around.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 若蝉蜕视镜如婴孩之貌 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'if one looks into the mirror as if seeing the appearance of an infant,' suggesting a transformation or a return to a simpler state.
  • 霓旌羽盖 - Refers to a colorful flag and a feathered canopy, symbols of divine or supernatural presence.
  • 仙乐步虚 - Refers to celestial music and the act of walking in a trance-like state, often associated with spiritual ascension.
  • 真人空际 - Means 'true people in the void,' referring to immortals or enlightened beings in the spiritual realm.
  • 崔郎 - A term of endearment or a nickname, possibly referring to a specific individual named Cui.
  • 翩翻于云汉之内 - Poetic phrase meaning 'flitting within the clouds,' describing the movement of the immortals.
  • 嬉游佚乐 - Means 'playing and enjoying oneself,' describing a carefree and joyful state.
  • 血属要与一诀 - Formal expression meaning 'to take leave of one's family,' emphasizing the gravity of the decision.
  • 不得淹留 - Means 'cannot linger,' indicating the urgency of the situation.
  • 谴罪极大 - Means 'punishment is severe,' highlighting the consequences of staying too long.
  • 朱书照之 - Refers to using a red inscription or talisman to reveal something hidden.
  • 临别更与符一道 - Means 'at the moment of departure, give another talisman,' indicating a final piece of advice or protection.
  • 甚急即开 - Means 'open it immediately if in great danger,' emphasizing the importance of following instructions quickly.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 若蝉蜕 - Reference to the mythological transformation of a cicada, symbolizing a change in state or a return to a purer form.
  • 仙伯 - Refers to a senior immortal or a divine being, often associated with longevity and wisdom.
  • 蘂宫 - Refers to the Palace of Flowers, a mythical place in Chinese mythology where immortals reside.

  • 锦绣 - Refers to luxurious brocade or silk, symbolizing wealth and luxury.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 若蝉蜕视镜如婴孩之貌 is metaphorical and requires careful translation to convey the intended imagery.
  • 霓旌羽盖仙乐步虚与妻相见 is a long sequence describing a series of actions and elements that must be read together to understand the scene.
  • 真人空际皆以崔郎为戏 indicates that the immortals are playing with someone named Cui, but the exact nature of the play is unclear.
  • 不得淹留谴罪极大 emphasizes the severity of the punishment for lingering.
  • 甚急即开却令取所乘驴付之 is a sequence of actions that must be read as a single unit, indicating the urgency and specific instructions given.

Proper Nouns

  • 崔郎 - Likely refers to a specific individual named Cui.
  • 仙伯 - A title for a senior immortal.
  • 蘂宫 - The Palace of Flowers, a mythical place.
  • 太真妃 - Consort Yang, a historical figure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 上不信令笞死忽记仙翁临行之符 - The emperor did not believe the order to whip Feng Lang to death and suddenly remembered the talisman given by the immortal on his departure.
  • 遽发公逺与捉者皆僵仆良久能起 - Rushed to send a distant official and those who caught him all fell to the ground and remained motionless for a long time before being able to rise.
  • 此已居上界杀之必不得假使得之臣軰便受祸亦非国家之福 - This one has already ascended to the upper realm; killing him is impossible. Even if we were to catch him, we would suffer misfortune, and it would not be a blessing for the state.
  • 乃释之亲召与语曰 - He then released him and personally summoned him to speak, saying.
  • 汝莫妄居遂令百人具兵仗同卫士同送且觇其故 - Do not rashly occupy [this place]. I will have a hundred men with weapons accompany the guards to escort you and observe what happens.
  • 却至洞口复见金城綘阙仙伯严侍卫出门呼曰 - As they returned to the cave entrance, they saw again the golden city and vermilion gate, with the Immortal Lord and strict attendants. The Immortal Lord called out.
  • 崔郎不记吾言几至颠踬 - Feng Lang, do you not remember my words? You nearly fell into danger.
  • 崔生拜讫将前送者亦欲随至 - Feng Lang bowed and was about to go forward, and the escorts also wanted to follow.
  • 仙翁以杖画成涧深濶各数丈 - The Immortal Lord drew a deep and wide ravine with his staff.
  • 令召崔生妻至掷一领巾过作五色防桥遣生登随步即灭 - He ordered Feng Lang's wife to be summoned and threw her a scarf that became a colorful bridge, sending Feng Lang to cross. As he stepped on it, it vanished.
  • 既度崔生回首曰即如此可以归矣 - After crossing, Feng Lang turned back and said, 'If it is like this, we can return.'
  • 须臾云雾四起咫尺不见唯闻鸾笙歌之声半日方散遥望惟空山而已不复有物也 - Soon, clouds and mists rose, making it impossible to see anything within a short distance. Only the sounds of phoenixes and singing could be heard, and it took half a day for them to disperse. From afar, only an empty mountain could be seen, with nothing else.

  • 鸾笙歌 - Refers to the sounds of phoenixes and music, often associated with heavenly or divine realms.
  • 逸史 - Refers to a collection of historical anecdotes or stories, often containing fantastical elements.

  • 金城 - Golden city.
  • 綘阙 - Vermilion gate.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是浔阳人要当时至以此便相假岂为辞耶 - A somewhat ambiguous phrase, likely a question or statement about someone from Xunyang being asked to lend something, implying refusal is not an option.
  • 舟人遂拜受之而去 - '舟人' refers to boatmen or rowers. The phrase means 'the boatmen then bowed and accepted it before leaving.'
  • 实江州人也 - '江州' is another place name, likely referring to Jiangzhou. The phrase means 'in fact, he was from Jiangzhou.'
  • 遂引俊药于乱石间行五六里 - '引俊药' is unclear; it might refer to guiding someone (俊) to a place where medicine is stored or administered. '行五六里' means 'walked about five or six miles.'
  • 将至山下有一大石方数丈 - '方数丈' means 'square in several zhang' (an ancient Chinese unit of measurement).
  • 道士以小石扣之数十下大石分为二 - '道士' means 'Taoist priest.' The phrase describes a Taoist priest using a small stone to strike a large stone, causing it to split into two.
  • 有一童出于石间喜曰尊师归也 - '童' means 'child' or 'young person.' The phrase describes a young person emerging from between the stones and saying, 'Master, you have returned.'
  • 道士遂引俊入石穴初甚峻下十丈余旁行渐寛平入数十歩其中洞明 - '石穴' means 'stone cave.' The phrase describes the Taoist priest leading the person into a stone cave, which is initially steep but becomes wider and flatter as they descend.
  • 有大石堂道士数十奕棊戏笑 - '大石堂' means 'large stone hall.' The phrase describes a large stone hall where many Taoist priests are playing chess and laughing.
  • 见道士皆曰何晚也 - The phrase means 'they all said, 'Why so late?''
  • 敕俊舍药命左右速遣来人归前道士命左右曰担人甚饥与之饭食 - '敕' means 'ordered.' The phrase describes the Taoist priest ordering the person to leave the medicine and instructing his attendants to quickly send the messenger back. He then orders his attendants to give the messenger food because they are very hungry.
  • 遂于瓷瓯盛胡麻饭与之食又与一碗浆甘滑如乳不知何物也 - '瓷瓯' means 'porcelain bowl.' The phrase describes the Taoist priest giving the messenger a bowl of sesame rice and a bowl of sweet, smooth liquid, possibly milk.
  • 又问家有几口云妻儿五口授以丹药可百余粒曰日食一粒 - The phrase describes the Taoist priest asking about the number of family members and giving the person over a hundred pills, instructing them to take one each day.

  • 道士 - Refers to Taoist priests, who were often associated with alchemy and medicine in ancient China.
  • 石穴 - A stone cave, often associated with Taoist hermits or hidden treasures.
  • 胡麻饭 - Sesame rice, a traditional Chinese dish.
  • 丹药 - Refers to elixirs or pills, often associated with Taoist alchemy and longevity.

  • 石穴 - Stone cave.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 可百日不食 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'can go without food for a hundred days,' indicating extreme endurance.
  • 此归路逺何由可知道士曰与汝图之遂引行乱石间 - Elliptical phrase requiring interpretation: 'Since the return path is far, how can we know the way? The hermit said to plan with you, then led the way through the rocks.'
  • 见一石卧如虎状令俊骑上以物蒙石头俊执其末如执辔焉 - Describes a stone resembling a tiger, which the hermit made the man ride, covering the stone with something and making the man hold its end like holding reins.
  • 诫令闭目候足着地即开俊如言骑石道士以鞭鞭石遂觉此石举在空中而飞 - The hermit instructed the man to close his eyes and open them when his feet touched the ground. The man followed the instructions and rode the stone, which the hermit whipped, causing it to rise into the air and fly.
  • 广陵郭门 - Refers to the city gate of Guangling.
  • 解腰下皆金钱也 - Means 'removed from his waist, all were coins of gold.'
  • 里人皆疑为盗也 - Means 'The villagers all suspected him of being a thief.'

  • 帑藏钱帛颇有侵用 - Means 'there is quite a bit of embezzlement in the treasury and storage of money and silk.'
  • 促命酒连举数杯 - Means 'quickly order wine and drink several cups in succession.'
  • 素少是日亦挹三四爵殊不觉醉心甚异之 - Means 'usually drink little, but on this day, after drinking three or four cups, one does not feel drunk at all, which is very unusual.'
  • 月甚明朗 - Means 'the moon is very bright and clear.'
  • 阖扉而出 - Means 'close the door and come out.'
  • 子有道骨法当度世 - Means 'you have the bones of a Daoist and should transcend the world.'
  • 加以笃尚正直性无忿恚 - Means 'furthermore, you are steadfast in uprightness and have no anger or resentment.'
  • 仙家尤重此行 - Means 'the Daoist realm especially values this behavior.'
  • 吾太上真人也 - Means 'I am a True Person of the Supreme Dao.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 方伯 - Refers to a high-ranking official, typically a governor or a regional administrator.
  • 帑藏 - Refers to the treasury or storage of state funds.
  • 钱帛 - Refers to money and silk, which were common forms of wealth in ancient China.
  • 铛釜铁器 - Refers to various cooking utensils and tools.
  • 药室 - Refers to a room or place where medicines are prepared or stored.
  • 道骨 - Refers to the physical and spiritual characteristics of a Daoist.
  • 度世 - Means 'to transcend the world' or 'to achieve immortality.'
  • 太上真人 - Refers to a high-ranking Daoist sage or immortal.

  • 叟 - A term for an old man or elder.
  • 太上真人 - A title for a high-ranking Daoist sage or immortal.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 游观人间以度有心之士 - To wander through the world to measure the minds of those with aspirations; suggests a philosophical or spiritual journey.
  • 悯子勤志 - To pity your diligent aspirations; conveys compassion for someone's hard work and dedication.
  • 化黄白之物 - To transform yellow and white substances; likely refers to alchemy, where base metals are transformed into gold or silver.
  • 遗子孙旁济贫乏 - To leave for descendants and to aid the poor on the side; indicates a dual purpose: inheritance and charity.
  • 支纳帑藏无贻后忧 - To support the treasury without leaving future worries; suggests financial prudence and foresight.
  • 命棹游江 - To command the oars to sail the river; is a metaphorical instruction to set out on a journey.
  • 中流 - Midstream; refers to the middle of a river, often used metaphorically to indicate a critical point or decision.
  • 若山凌晨开阅所化之物 - If Mountain opens and reads the transformed substances at dawn; suggests a moment of revelation or inspection.
  • 烂然照屋 - Shining brightly to illuminate the house; describes something that is radiant or impressive.
  • 扃闭之 - To lock it up; indicates securing or storing something.
  • 整棹浮江 - To prepare the oars and float on the river; is another metaphorical instruction to set out on a journey.
  • 超然而去 - To depart with a sense of transcendence; suggests a departure that is beyond the ordinary.
  • 风波稍定 - The waves and winds have slightly calmed; indicates a moment of relative calm after a turbulent period.
  • 诀别之书 - A farewell letter; is a letter written before parting, often containing final words or instructions.
  • 指挥家事 - To direct family affairs; indicates managing or overseeing household matters.
  • 遗表 - A will or testament; is a document outlining one's wishes after death.
  • 奏闻 - To report to the emperor; indicates a formal communication to a ruler.
  • 世禄暂荣 - Temporary glory of hereditary nobility; refers to the fleeting nature of noble status.
  • 浮生难保 - The floating life is hard to preserve; suggests the impermanence of life.
  • 登真脱屣 - To ascend to truth and discard shoes; is a metaphor for achieving spiritual enlightenment and leaving behind worldly concerns.
  • 后天为期 - To set the date for the afterlife; indicates a focus on the spiritual or afterlife realm.
  • 范丞相泛舟五湖 - Chancellor Fan sailed on the Five Lakes; is a historical reference to a nobleman who chose to live in seclusion.
  • 张留侯去师四皓 - Zhang Liuhou left to follow the Four Sages; is a reference to Zhang Liang, a famous strategist who sought spiritual guidance.
  • 不堪同乐 - Cannot share in the joy; suggests a lack of compatibility or shared values.
  • 不可久存 - Cannot be kept for long; indicates the transient nature of certain relationships or situations.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 同臣运属休明 - Classical expression meaning 'the same as the minister's fortune belongs to the auspicious and bright era,' indicating a favorable time for the subject.
  • 累叨荣爵 - Means 'repeatedly receiving honors and titles,' referring to the accumulation of noble ranks.
  • 早悟升沉之理 - Means 'early understanding of the principles of rise and fall,' referring to the realization of the ups and downs in one's career.
  • 深知止足之规 - Means 'deeply understanding the rules of contentment,' indicating a profound awareness of the importance of knowing when to stop and be satisfied.
  • 偶得丹诀 - Means 'accidentally obtaining the secret of alchemy,' referring to the discovery of the secret formula for immortality.
  • 黄金可作 - Means 'gold can be made,' referring to the ability to transmute base metals into gold.
  • 信淮王之昔言 - Means 'believing the words of the Duke of Huai in the past,' referring to the historical figure who was said to have achieved immortality.
  • 白日可延 - Means 'extending life to the day,' referring to the possibility of living indefinitely.
  • 察真经之妙用 - Means 'understanding the wondrous effects of the true scriptures,' referring to the profound benefits of spiritual texts.
  • 既得之矣余复何求 - Means 'having obtained it, what else do I seek?' indicating a sense of fulfillment and contentment.
  • 是用挥手红尘 - Means 'thus waving goodbye to the mundane world,' referring to renouncing worldly affairs.
  • 腾神碧海 - Means 'ascending the spirit to the azure sea,' referring to the spiritual journey to the heavens.
  • 扶桑在望 - Means 'the land of Fusang is in sight,' referring to a mythical place.
  • 蓬岛非遥 - Means 'the island of Penglai is not far,' referring to another mythical place associated with immortality.
  • 遐瞻帝阍 - Means 'looking afar at the imperial gate,' referring to the emperor's court.
  • 不胜犬马恋主之至 - Means 'unable to bear the deep affection of a dog and horse for their master,' expressing a strong sense of loyalty and attachment.
  • 遽命优恤其家 - Means 'immediately ordered generous care for his family,' indicating the emperor's kindness.
  • 促召唐若水 - Means 'hurriedly summoned Tang Ruoshui,' referring to the summoning of a specific individual.
  • 与内臣赍诏于江表海濵 - Means 'along with an eunuch, carrying the imperial decree to the southern regions by the sea,' referring to the delivery of the emperor's message.
  • 寻访杳无音尘 - Means 'searched for but found no trace,' indicating the failure to locate someone.
  • 其后二十年 - Means 'twenty years later,' marking the passage of time.
  • 有若山旧吏 - Means 'there was an old official of Ruoshan,' referring to a former servant of Tang Ruoshui.
  • 自浙西奉使淮南 - Means 'from the western part of Zhejiang, serving as an envoy to Huainan,' referring to the official's role.
  • 于鱼市中见若山鬻鱼于肆 - Means 'saw Ruoshan selling fish in the market,' describing the encounter.
  • 混同常人 - Means 'blending in with ordinary people,' indicating that Tang Ruoshui was indistinguishable from others.
  • 睨其吏而延之入陋巷中 - Means 'glanced at the official and invited him into a humble alley,' describing the interaction.
  • 萦廻数百歩乃及华第 - Means 'winding around for hundreds of steps until reaching a splendid mansion,' describing the journey to the grand house.
  • 止吏与食 - Means 'stopped the official and offered him food,' indicating hospitality.
  • 哀其久贫命市铁二十梃 - Means 'pitied his long-term poverty and ordered twenty iron bars to be bought,' showing compassion.
  • 明日复与相遇 - Means 'the next day, they met again,' continuing the narrative.
  • 已化金矣 - Means 'had already turned into gold,' referring to the transformation of the iron bars.
  • 尽以遗之 - Means 'gave all of it to him,' indicating generosity.
  • 姓刘今刘子孙世居金陵 - Means 'his surname was Liu, and his descendants now live in Jinling,' referring to the lineage.
  • 亦有修道者 - Means 'there are also those who practice Daoism,' indicating a continuation of spiritual pursuits.
  • 又相国李绅字公垂常习业于华山 - Means 'also, the chancellor Li Sen, with the courtesy name Gongchi, often studied on Mount Hua,' referring to a historical figure.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 若山 - Refers to Tang Ruoshan, a historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 扶桑 - A mythical land in Chinese mythology, often associated with the eastern sea.
  • 蓬岛 - Another mythical land, specifically the island of Penglai, known for its association with immortality.
  • 淮王 - Refers to the Duke of Huai, a historical figure associated with the legend of alchemy and immortality.
  • 华山 - One of the Five Great Mountains in China, known for its spiritual significance and as a place of Daoist practice.
  • 李绅 - A historical figure, chancellor during the Tang Dynasty, known for his literary and political contributions.

  • 华山 - Mount Hua.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 廣又入一门屋宇甚大 - Guang again entered a large hall; '又入' (again entered) indicates a repeated action.
  • 揖元瓌于门下 - Bowed to Yuan Gui at the door; '揖' (bow) is a formal gesture of respect.
  • 先入为席良久出迎 - Entered first and sat down for a long time before coming out to greet; '先入' (entered first) and '良久' (for a long time) indicate a sequence of actions.
  • 容状伟烁 - Appearance and demeanor were grand and brilliant; '伟烁' (grand and brilliant) describes an impressive appearance.
  • 可年二十许 - Could be around twenty years old; '许' (around) indicates an approximate age.
  • 云冠霞衣 - Cloud cap and rosy robe; '云冠' (cloud cap) and '霞衣' (rosy robe) are symbolic of celestial attire.
  • 左右玉童侍女三五十軰 - On both sides were thirty or fifty jade boys and maidens; '三五十軰' (thirty or fifty) is a numerical approximation.
  • 皆非世所有 - All were not of this world; '世' (this world) contrasts the otherworldly nature of the beings.
  • 元瓌莫之测 - Yuan Gui could not fathom it; '莫之测' (could not fathom) indicates confusion or inability to understand.
  • 相引升堂 - Led him up to the hall; '相引' (led) and '升堂' (up to the hall) describe a guided ascent.
  • 虽王者宴赐亦所不及 - Even the banquets of kings could not match them; '王者' (kings) and '宴赐' (banquets) emphasize the grandeur.
  • 君与妻同坐 - The host and his wife sat together; '君' (host) and '妻' (wife) indicate a couple.
  • 不可令侍御独坐 - It would not be right to let the attendant sit alone; '侍御' (attendant) and '独坐' (sit alone) suggest a social norm.
  • 即召一人坐于元瓌之侧 - He immediately called one person to sit beside Yuan Gui; '即召' (immediately called) and '坐于' (sit beside) describe a specific action.
  • 元瓌视之乃其妻也 - Yuan Gui looked and saw that it was his wife; '视之' (looked) and '乃其妻也' (it was his wife) clarify the identity.
  • 奏乐酣饮 - Music was played and they drank heartily; '奏乐' (played music) and '酣饮' (drank heartily) describe the atmosphere.
  • 既醉各散 - After getting drunk, they dispersed; '既醉' (after getting drunk) and '各散' (dispersed) indicate the end of the gathering.
  • 终不及相问言情 - They never got around to asking about each other's feelings; '终不及' (never got around to) and '相问言情' (asking about each other's feelings) suggest a missed opportunity.
  • 迟明告别 - They bid farewell at dawn; '迟明' (at dawn) and '告别' (bid farewell) describe the timing of the departure.
  • 君赠元瓌金尺玉鞭 - The host gave Yuan Gui a golden ruler and a jade whip; '赠' (gave) and '金尺玉鞭' (golden ruler and jade whip) describe the gifts.
  • 出门行数里 - They went out and walked several miles; '出门' (went out) and '行数里' (walked several miles) describe the journey.
  • 因使人访其处 - They then sent someone to inquire about the place; '因使人' (then sent someone) and '访其处' (inquire about the place) describe a subsequent action.
  • 无复踪迹矣 - There were no traces left; '无复' (no longer) and '踪迹' (traces) indicate the disappearance of the place.
  • 及还京问其妻曾有异事乎 - Upon returning to the capital, he asked his wife if there had been any strange events; '及还京' (upon returning to the capital) and '曾有异事乎' (if there had been any strange events) describe the inquiry.
  • 具言某日昬然思睡 - She said that on a certain day she felt drowsy; '具言' (said) and '昬然思睡' (felt drowsy) describe the memory.
  • 有黑衣人来称司命君召某便随去 - A man in black came and said that the Lord of Fate had summoned her, so she followed him; '黑衣人' (man in black), '司命君' (Lord of Fate), and '召某便随去' (summoned her, so she followed him) describe the encounter.
  • 既至司命宫中 - Upon arriving at the Palace of Fate; '既至' (upon arriving) and '司命宫中' (Palace of Fate) describe the location.
  • 见与君同饮 - She saw that she was drinking with the host; '见与' (saw that) and '同饮' (drinking) describe the scene.
  • 后十年元瓌奉使江岭 - Ten years later, Yuan Gui was sent on a mission to Jiangling; '后十年' (ten years later) and '奉使江岭' (sent on a mission to Jiangling) describe the timeline.
  • 又于江西泊舟 - He again docked his boat in Jiangxi; '又于' (again) and '泊舟' (docked his boat) describe the location.
  • 见君在岸上邀入一草堂 - He saw the host on the shore inviting him into a thatched cottage; '见君' (saw the host), '邀入' (inviting him into), and '一草堂' (a thatched cottage) describe the scene.

  • 江岭 - A region in southern China, historically significant for its natural beauty and strategic importance.

  • 自此叙别不复再见亦不知司命所主何事所修何道品位仙秩定何髙卑复何姓字耳 - Long phrase meaning 'from this point on, they parted ways and never saw each other again; he also didn't know what the official in charge of life and death was responsible for, what path he was cultivating, what rank he held in the celestial hierarchy, or his surname and given name.'

  • 此天帝流华寳爵耳致于日中则白气连天承以玉盘则红光照室 - Phrase meaning 'This is the Heavenly Emperor's Flowing Beauty Jewel Cup; when placed in the sun, it emits a white vapor that reaches the sky; when placed on a jade plate, it emits a red light that fills the room.'
  • 此寳太上西北库中镇中华二十四寳也顷年已旋降今此第二十二寳亦不久留于人间即当飞去 - Phrase meaning 'This treasure is one of the twenty-four treasures that the Supreme One keeps in the northwest treasury to govern the Central Plains; it has recently descended to earth, and this is the twenty-second treasure; it will not stay on earth for long and will soon fly away.'
  • 得此寳者受福七世敬之哉元瓌以玉盘承之夜视红光满室 - Phrase meaning 'The person who possesses this treasure will receive blessings for seven generations; revere it! Yuan Gui placed it on a jade plate, and at night, the red light filled the room.'

  • 善画 - Literally 'good at painting,' meaning skilled in painting.

  • 卧雪不寒 - Literally 'sleeping on snow without feeling cold,' meaning to be unaffected by harsh conditions.

  • 天下山水皆所游览 - Literally 'all mountains and waters of the world have been visited,' meaning to have traveled extensively and seen many natural landscapes.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 白云乘马与从者四十余人走于汉水之上 - Describes a scene where the immortal Liu Baiyun rides a horse with over forty followers, crossing the Han River.
  • 蹙波起尘如履平地 - Idiom meaning 'crushing waves and raising dust as if walking on level ground,' indicating supernatural speed and ease.
  • 追之不得 - Means 'cannot be caught up with,' emphasizing the immortality's elusiveness.
  • 谓追者曰 - Introduces a direct speech, meaning 'told those who were pursuing him.'
  • 我刘白云也 - Reveals the identity of the immortal, meaning 'I am Liu Baiyun.'
  • 后于江西湖南人多见之 - Indicates widespread sightings of the immortal, meaning 'later, many people in Jiangxi and Hunan saw him.'
  • 弥更年少洁白 - Describes the immortal's appearance, meaning 'he became even younger and whiter.'
  • 时湖南刺史王逊好道 - Provides background on the historical figure, meaning 'at that time, the governor of Hunan, Wang Xun, was fond of Daoism.'
  • 白云时来郡中 - Indicates frequent visits, meaning 'Baiyun often came to the prefecture.'
  • 忽一日别去 - Describes an unexpected departure, meaning 'one day, he suddenly left.'
  • 谓逊曰 - Introduces another direct speech, meaning 'told Wang Xun.'
  • 将往洪州即于钟陵相见 - Indicates a future meeting, meaning 'I will go to Hongzhou, and we will meet at Zhongling.'
  • 一揖而行 - Describes a formal farewell, meaning 'gave a bow and left.'
  • 初不晓其防 - Indicates confusion, meaning 'initially, no one understood his intention.'
  • 辰发灵州午时已在湘潭 - Emphasizes the incredible speed, meaning 'he left Lingzhou in the morning and arrived in Xiangtan by noon.'
  • 人多识者验其所行顷刻七百里矣 - Confirms the supernatural speed, meaning 'many people who recognized him verified that he had travelled seven hundred li in an instant.'
  • 旬日王逊果除洪州到任后白云亦来相访 - Indicates a sequence of events, meaning 'after ten days, Wang Xun was indeed appointed governor of Hongzhou. After taking office, Baiyun also came to visit.'
  • 复于江都值乐真人曰 - Introduces another direct speech, meaning 'later, in Jiangdu, he met the immortal Le, who said.'
  • 尔周游人间固有年矣 - Acknowledges the immortal's long-term presence, meaning 'you have been wandering in the human world for many years.'
  • 金液九丹之经太上所敇令授于尔 - Indicates a divine command, meaning 'the *Jin Ye Jiu Dan Jing* (Classic of the Nine Golden Elixirs) has been commanded by the Supreme One to be given to you.'
  • 可名岳福地链而服之千日之外可以登云天矣 - Describes a spiritual practice, meaning 'you can name and consecrate a mountain as a blessed land, practice alchemy, and consume it. After a thousand days, you can ascend to the clouds and heavens.'
  • 干符中犹在长安市卖药 - Indicates the continued presence, meaning 'during the Gan Fu period, he was still selling medicine in Chang'an.'
  • 人有识之者但不可亲炙无由师匠耳 - Indicates the difficulty of interacting with the immortal, meaning 'some people recognized him, but they could not approach him closely or become his disciples.'
  • 【出仙传拾遗】 - Provides the source of the text, meaning 'from *Xian Chuan Shi Yi* (Supplementary Records of Immortals).'

  • 性颇好道 - His nature was quite fond of Daoism or his disposition was inclined toward the Dao.
  • 以金丹延生为务 - Made it his duty to pursue the elixir of immortality to prolong life. '金丹' refers to the alchemical elixir of immortality; '延生' means to prolong life.
  • 鑪鼎所费家无余财 - The cost of the furnace and crucible left the family with no surplus wealth. '鑪鼎' refers to the alchemical furnace and crucible used in the preparation of the elixir.
  • 官散俸薄 - His official salary was meager and his position was no longer active. '官散' may mean that his official post was no longer in effect or was reduced.
  • 往往阙于饘粥 - Frequently lacking even the most basic sustenance (rice porridge). '饘粥' refers to simple, nourishing food.
  • 稍有百金即输于炭药之直矣 - Whenever he had a hundred gold coins, he would immediately spend them on charcoal and medicine. '炭药' refers to the materials used in alchemy.
  • 金丹之制不足为劳 - The preparation of the elixir is not worth the labor. '制' here means to prepare or create.
  • 黄白变化咳唾可致 - The transformation of gold and silver can be achieved by mere breath or spittle. This is a metaphor for alchemical transformation being easily achieved.
  • 凡之刚躁諠杂嗜酒贪饕殊不可耐 - Fan was brash, loud, and noisy; he loved alcohol and was gluttonous—very difficult to endure. '刚躁' means brash or impetuous; '諠杂' means noisy or boisterous; '嗜酒贪饕' means addicted to alcohol and gluttony.
  • 昼出夜还不畏街禁 - He went out during the day and returned at night, without fearing the street curfews. '街禁' refers to curfews or restrictions on movement in the streets.
  • 肥鲜醇酎非时即须 - He required rich, fresh, and strong wine at any time. '肥鲜' refers to rich and fresh food; '醇酎' refers to strong or aged wine.
  • 了谙其性委曲预备必副所求 - He thoroughly understood Fan’s nature, prepared everything meticulously, and always fulfilled his desires. '委曲' here means to prepare carefully or with great attention to detail.
  • 凡自击碎之垒铁加炭 - Fan himself smashed them and piled iron on top of charcoal. This may refer to an alchemical process or a symbolic act of destruction and transformation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隋太子勇之孙名徳祖 - The grandson of Yang Jian, the Prince of the Sui dynasty, was named Dezu. This refers to Yang Jian, the founder of the Sui dynasty, and his son Yang Yong (the Prince of Yong), who was deposed and later executed. Dezu was his grandson, likely from a later generation.
  • 仕唐为尚辇奉御 - Served in the Tang dynasty as a *shangnian fengyu*. This was a low-ranking official position in the Tang court, responsible for the imperial carriages and ceremonial duties.
  • 金丹延生 - The elixir of immortality to prolong life. This is a reference to Daoist alchemy, a common practice in the Tang dynasty, especially among those seeking longevity or immortality.
  • 仙籍 - The registry of immortals. In Daoist tradition, there was a belief that certain individuals could be recorded in the 'immortal registry' after achieving immortality or spiritual transcendence.
  • 黄白变化 - The transformation of gold and silver. This refers to the alchemical goal of transmuting base metals into gold, a key goal in Daoist and Western alchemy.
  • 街禁 - Street curfews. In the Tang dynasty, there were strict regulations on movement during the night, especially in the capital, to maintain order and security.
  • 肥鲜醇酎 - Rich, fresh, and strong wine. This reflects the luxurious and indulgent lifestyle of the elite, particularly those engaged in Daoist or alchemical pursuits.

Proper Nouns

  • 凡八兄 - A personal name or nickname. Likely a pseudonym or a title used in the text. The meaning of '凡八' is unclear, but it may be a numerical or symbolic reference.
  • 隋太子勇 - The Prince of the Sui dynasty, Yang Yong. He was the eldest son of Emperor Wen of Sui and was deposed and later executed.
  • 徳祖 - A personal name. Likely a historical figure or a fictional character in the text.
  • 唐 - The Tang dynasty, a major Chinese dynasty that ruled from 618 to 907 CE.
  • 尚辇奉御 - A low-ranking official title in the Tang court, responsible for the imperial carriages and ceremonial duties.
  • 金丹 - The elixir of immortality, a key concept in Daoist alchemy.
  • 仙籍 - The registry of immortals, a concept in Daoist cosmology.
  • 黄白 - Refers to gold and silver, the goal of alchemical transmutation.
  • 街禁 - Street curfews or restrictions on movement, common in the Tang dynasty.

  • 服色叹息数四乃借帔观之则知非人间物试之水火亦不焚汚因留诘之 - A compound sentence describing a series of actions and observations leading to the realization that the item is supernatural.
  • 李具陈本末 - Means that Li explained the beginning and end of the story.

  • 章仇意疑仙者往来必在药肆 - Means that Zhang Qiu suspected that the immortals must frequent the pharmacy.
  • 因令药师候其出处 - Means that he ordered the pharmacist to wait and observe their comings and goings.
  • 居四日得之 - Means that after four days, they found something.
  • 初有小童诣肆市药 - Means that initially, a young boy came to the pharmacy to buy medicine.

  • 以恶药与之 - Means that he gave the boy bad medicine.
  • 小童往而复来且嘱云大人怒药不佳欲见捶挞 - Means that the boy went and returned, saying that the adults were angry because the medicine was bad and wanted to beat him.
  • 童子云许老翁也 - Means that the boy said it was Mr. Xu.
  • 药师甚喜引童白府章仇令劲徤百人卒吏五十人随童诣山 - Means that the pharmacist was very happy and led the boy to report to the governor, Zhang Qiu, who then sent 100 soldiers and 50 officials to follow the boy to the mountain.
  • 且申令山峯巉絶众莫能上 - Means that the governor also ordered that the mountain peak was steep and no one could climb it.
  • 童乃自下大呼须臾老翁出石壁上问何故领尔许人来 - Means that the boy called out from below, and soon, Mr. Xu appeared on the stone wall and asked why so many people had come.

  • 有大貍腰斩死于牀下 - There was a large fox that was beheaded and died under the bed.
  • 疾乃止 - The illness then ceased.
  • 时杜暹为婺州叅军与贾同列相得甚欢 - At that time, Du Xian was a military officer in Wuzhou and was on the same rank as Jia; they got along very well.
  • 与暹同部领使于洛阳过钱塘江登罗刹山观浙江潮 - He traveled with Du Xian to Luoyang, passed through the Qiantang River, climbed Mount Luoshan, and watched the tide of the Qiantang River.
  • 大禹真圣者当以水时所有金柜玉符以镇川渎 - The Great Yu is truly a sage; during the time of water, he had gold cabinets and jade talismans to pacify the rivers and streams.
  • 若此杭州城不镇压寻当防矣 - If this is not done, Hangzhou City will soon be in danger.
  • 暹曰何以知之 - Du Xian said, 'How do you know this?'
  • 贾曰此石下是相与观焉 - Jia said, 'This stone is what we observed together.'
  • 因令暹闭目执其手令暹跳下 - So he made Du Xian close his eyes, take his hand, and jump down.
  • 暹忽闭目已至水防其空处如堂有大石柜髙丈余鏁之 - Du Xian suddenly found himself with closed eyes, having already reached the water, with a large stone cabinet as high as over ten feet, locked.
  • 贾手开其鏁去其盖引暹手登之 - Jia opened the lock with his hand, removed the lid, and led Du Xian's hand to climb up.
  • 因同入柜中又有金柜可髙三尺金鏁鎻之 - Then they both entered the cabinet; there was another gold cabinet as high as three feet, locked with a gold lock.
  • 贾曰玉符在中然世人不合见 - Jia said, 'The jade talisman is inside, but it is not meant for ordinary people to see.'
  • 暹观 - Du Xian observed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 女病魅 - Refers to a woman being possessed by a spirit, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 桃符 - Peachwood charms, traditionally used in Chinese culture to ward off evil spirits.
  • 大禹 - The legendary ruler Yu, known for his efforts to control floods and establish the Xia Dynasty.
  • 金柜玉符 - Gold cabinets and jade talismans, symbolic of power and protection in Chinese mythology.
  • 罗刹山 - Mount Luoshan, a location mentioned in Chinese literature and folklore.
  • 浙江潮 - The tide of the Qiantang River, famous for its dramatic tidal waves.

[400]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既已又接其手令腾出暹距跃则至岸矣 - Describes a sequence of actions: 'having done so, he took her hand and made her jump, and she reached the shore.'
  • 君有宰相禄当自保爱 - Direct statement advising the person to take care of themselves as they are destined for a high position.
  • 因示其拜官厯任及于年寿周细语之 - 'He then showed her the details of his official appointments, years of service, and lifespan, speaking them out in detail.'
  • 所以早天妻崔氏亦非吾妻即吉州别驾李乙妻也 - Explains why the wife died young and clarifies that she was not the speaker's wife but the wife of Li Yi, the Magistrate of Jizhou.
  • 縁时嵗未到乙未合娶以世人亦合有室故司命权以妻吾 - Explains that because the time had not yet come for Li Yi to marry, the goddess temporarily gave him her wife.
  • 吾今期尽妻即当过李氏 - Indicates that the speaker's time is up and his wife will return to Li Yi.
  • 吾第三天人也有罪谪为世人二十五年今已满矣后日当行 - Explains that the speaker is a third-tier immortal who was punished to be a human for 25 years, and now his term is over.

[401]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至洛得遂栖息 - Literally means 'reached Luoyang and settled down,' but can be interpreted as 'arrived in Luoyang and began to settle in.'
  • 行路之人犹合矜愍 - Means 'those who travel should still be pitied,' emphasizing compassion for travelers.
  • 室家之好而忽遗弃耶 - Means 'how can one suddenly abandon the affection of one's family?' expressing the moral duty to care for one's family.
  • 贾笑而不答 - Means 'Jia smiled without answering,' indicating a non-verbal response.
  • 因令造棺器纳亡女其中寘之船下 - Means 'then ordered to make a coffin and place the deceased daughter inside, placing it under the boat,' describing a specific action.
  • 又嘱暹以身后事 - Means 'also entrusted Xian with his after-death affairs,' indicating a final request.
  • 吾卒后为素棺漆其缝 - Means 'after I die, make a plain coffin and paint the seams,' providing specific instructions.
  • 将至先茔与女子皆袝于墓 - Means 'when I reach my ancestral tomb, both I and the girl will be buried there,' indicating a joint burial.
  • 殓后即发使至宋州崔氏伯任宋州别驾 - Means 'after the burial, immediately send a messenger to Cui's uncle, who is the governor of Song Prefecture,' describing a subsequent action.
  • 当留其侄聴之 - Means 'he should keep his nephew, Tong,' indicating a request to retain someone.
  • 至冬初李乙必充计入京与崔氏伯相见 - Means 'by early winter, Li Yi will surely be sent as an envoy to the capital to meet Cui's uncle,' predicting a future event.
  • 因求婚崔别驾以侄妻之事已定矣 - Means 'because of this, he will propose marriage to Cui's uncle, and the matter of marrying his nephew has been decided,' indicating a planned marriage.
  • 暹然之 - Means 'Xian agreed to it,' showing acceptance.
  • 其妻日夜涕泣请其少留终不答 - Means 'his wife wept day and night, asking him to stay a little longer, but he did not respond,' describing emotional pleading.
  • 至日沐浴衣新衣暮时召暹相对言谈顷而卧遂卒 - Means 'on that day, he bathed, wore new clothes, called Xian to talk, and after a short while, lay down and died,' describing the final moments.
  • 暹哭之恸为制朋友之服如其言殓之行及宋州崔别驾果留其侄暹至临汝乃厚葬贾及其女 - Means 'Xian cried bitterly, made mourning clothes for a friend, and followed his words to bury him. They reached Song Prefecture, and Cui's uncle indeed kept Tong. They then gave a grand burial to Jia and his daughter in Linru,' describing the aftermath.
  • 其冬李乙至宋州求壻其妻崔别驾以妻之暹后作相厯中外皆如其语 - Means 'that winter, Li Yi came to Song Prefecture to seek a son-in-law. Cui's uncle married him to his wife, and Xian later became a chancellor, serving both inside and outside the court, all as predicted,' indicating a fulfillment of prophecy.

  • 宋州 - Refers to Song Prefecture, a historical administrative region.

  • 宋州别驾 - Refers to the governor of Song Prefecture.
  • 临汝 - Refers to Linru, a place where the burial took place.
  • 相 - Refers to a high-ranking government official, specifically a chancellor.
  • 厯中外 - Refers to serving both inside and outside the court, indicating a wide range of political influence.

  • 琅琊临沂人也 - Refers to the place of origin as Linyi, Langya (琅琊临沂).
  • 北齐黄门侍郎之推五代孙 - Indicates that the person is the fifth-generation descendant of the Northern Qi official Huangmen Shilang Zhitui.

  • 有道士过其家自称北山君 - Describes a Taoist who visited the person's home and introduced himself as Beishan Jun (北山君).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雨郡人呼为御史雨 - Repetition of '雨' (rain) suggests a play on words or a pun, possibly a phonetic representation of a name or title.
  • 佯命文士泛舟饮酒赋诗 - Pretend to order scholars to cruise in boats, drink wine, and compose poetry.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 河东 - Hedong, a region in Shanxi Province, historically significant in Chinese history.
  • 郑延祚 - Historical figure whose mother's burial is mentioned.
  • 杨国忠 - Prominent official during the Tang Dynasty, known for his political influence.
  • 安禄山 - Military general who led the An Lushan Rebellion, a major uprising during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 李憕 - High-ranking official killed by An Lushan during the rebellion.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The repetition of '雨' in '雨郡人呼为御史雨' suggests a play on words, possibly a pun or a phonetic representation of a name or title.
  • The sentence '母卒二十九年殡于僧舍壖垣地' is elliptical, requiring the reader to infer that the mother died and was temporarily buried in a cemetery.
  • The sentence '兄弟三十年不齿天下耸动' implies that the brothers were disowned for 30 years, causing a significant stir.
  • The sentence '阴料丁壮实储廪佯命文士泛舟饮酒赋诗' describes a covert operation to assess and prepare for defense while pretending to engage in leisure activities.
  • The sentence '禄山既防洛阳杀留守李憕以其首招降河北' describes An Lushan's actions after the rebellion, including the killing of a high-ranking official and using his head to try to persuade others to surrender.
  • The sentence '真卿恐摇人心杀其使者乃谓诸将曰我识李憕此首非真也久之为冠饰以草续支体棺而葬之' describes Zhang Xu's actions to prevent panic by killing the messenger and presenting a fake head.

Proper Nouns

  • 郑延祚 - Historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 杨国忠 - High-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 安禄山 - Military general who led the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 李憕 - High-ranking official killed by An Lushan.
